news.title: KC crowd celebrates Spain’s win & love for soccer in final World Cup watch party
news.text:
For the last time this summer, thousands of people of all ages descended upon KC Live! inside the Power & Light District for the 2026 FIFA World Cup.

The outdoor entertainment center was the home of dozens of watch parties throughout the soccer tournament, branding their parties Soccer in the City. It captured both hope and heartbreak for the United States and Mexico fans, was the starting point of the Netherlands’ iconic Oranje Walk and provided one of many looks at Kansas City to the world when its gatherings were broadcast.

It all came to a close with the World Cup final Sunday, July 19. Attire representing both Spain and Argentina was worn by many, but others also took this opportunity to support their teams from all leagues and countries to celebrate soccer.

At first glance, the crowd looked like it was less in favor of one team and more hanging around for a good time. A call-and-response early on said otherwise, with the crowd cheering more for Spain than Argentina, although Argentina jerseys were the majority.

Either way, the twist and turns kept everyone on the edge of their feet (standing room only in the venue) for the duration of the game. Every foul, every push up the field and every shot on goal drew in everyone who braved the 90+ degree heat in the middle of a summer day.

The near-capacity KC Live! stuck it out for over two hours to see superstar Lamine Yamal and Spain claim their second World Cup and prevent Argentina, the last remaining team whose base camp was set in the Kansas City, from winning two World Cups in a row.

For David Dominguez, he was cheering for Spain since his father’s ancestors were born there. He was just happy to see Hispanic teams in the final.

“Even though I was rooting for Mexico, my brothers in Spain made it, too,” Dominguez said. “Credit to Argentina for making it as well.”

While he has ties to Spain, Dominguez said roots lie in the Dominican Republic since that’s where his parents were born. They opted to move to Kansas City for its soccer background.

Taking a look at the crowd cheering in excitement and tossing people in the air to celebrate the victory after the final whistle, Dominguez said his parents made the right call.

Spain came out on top 1-0 after Ferran Torres scored in the 106th minute. The shock of Spain’s goal sent the crowd into a frenzy, many of who had long been waiting to see the ball hit the back of the net. For others sporting blue and white, it brought them to pure silence knowing their chances of winning had dropped drastically.

Chino Montesinos felt bad for Lionel Messi, but that’s where it stopped. The California native who’s lived in Kansas City since 2011 was cheering for Spain this time since they only had one World Cup to their name before Sunday. While Montesinos was decked out in Spanish gear, he really just wanted a good final match to cap off the tournament.

He’d come down to P&L for every game featuring Mexico and Spain, and noted how even though there were people of both of the fandoms, he loved how everyone came together to have a good time.

He also pointed a few times when both sides reacted the same way: cheering when Jason Sudeikis as Ted Lasso and Justin Bieber appeared at the halftime show and booing when FIFA president Gianni Infantino and President Donald Trump were shown on the broadcast.

“People always talk about how the World Cup brings people together,” Montesinos said. “You can just look around, man. It’s amazing. It’s a beautiful experience.”

Montesinos is hopeful that the United States carries this momentum and appreciates soccer a little bit more, and he is looking forward to the next time the country hosts the FIFA World Cup, which will be the woman’s tournament in 2031.

Fans took turns climbing on each other’s shoulders to wave their flags high in the air after the win for Spain. When space cleared out from people leaving after the match ended, they took the opportunity to start dancing with each other as Spanish music blared through the speakers.

It wasn’t too long ago when the world was stunned to see Spain tie with Cabo Verde to open their World Cup campaign. Spain ended up playing the most complete tournament, only giving up one goal in its seven matches and many of its players earning best of the tournament honors.

The win meant a lot to everyone supporting Spain, including Kathryn Caneteren.

She’s currently living in Madrid, and came back to Kansas City for the summer to visit with friends and family. She was at the watch party with her brother, where they had both arrived nearly three hours before kickoff to secure a spot on the barricade.

Tears started to fill in her eyes when the broadcast showed what the atmosphere was like in Madrid. She wishes she could’ve been in both Madrid and Kansas City to experience the victory, but she doesn’t regret her choice.

“Vamos España, we just won the World Cup,” Caneteren said.

Not a bad way to close out the World Cup in the soccer capital of the U.S.
news.author: Joseph Hernandez

news.title: JD Vance and Usha Vance Welcome Fourth Child; Know Baby's Name Here
news.text:
US Vice President JD Vance and Second Lady Usha Vance announced the birth of their fourth child on Sunday, July 20, marking a rare milestone in American political history. Their son, Alec Neel Vance, is the first child born to a sitting US vice president in more than 150 years. The infant was born Sunday morning at the Walter Reed National Military Medical Center in Bethesda, Maryland. In a joint statement shared on social media, the couple expressed their joy regarding the new addition to their family.

“We are excited to announce that our baby boy, Alec Neel Vance, was born this morning. Usha and the baby are happy and healthy, and our kids are overjoyed to meet their little brother,” the statement read. The couple also extended their gratitude to the medical staff at Walter Reed and the White House Medical Unit for their care. Usha Vance Announces Pregnancy: JD Vance’s Wife and Second Lady of US Announces She’s Pregnant With 4th Child.

Usha and Baby Are Happy and Healthy, Says JD Vance

pic.twitter.com/VMCGCCRd7s— JD Vance (@JDVance) July 19, 2026

A Historic Addition

The birth of Alec Neel Vance represents a significant historical event, as it is the first time a sitting vice president has welcomed a child since 1870, when Schuyler Colfax, who served under President Ulysses S. Grant, became a father to his son, Schuyler Colfax III. Prior to that, Vice President John C. Calhoun welcomed a son in 1829. Alec joins the couple’s three older children: Ewan, 9, Vivek, 6, and Mirabel, 4. While the family has generally maintained privacy regarding their children, the vice president’s growing family has often been a topic of public interest, particularly given his vocal advocacy for policies encouraging larger families. Why JD Vance and Usha Vance Decided To Have a 4th Child After Charlie Kirk’s Death.

Context and Family Life

JD Vance, 41, and Usha Vance, 40, were married in 2014. The Second Lady, a former attorney who clerked for Supreme Court Chief Justice John Roberts, has frequently been involved in the couple's public life, including launching a video podcast titled Storytime with the Second Lady during her pregnancy. The arrival of their fourth child is part of a broader "mini baby boom" among top members of President Donald Trump’s administration. The Vances’ decision to expand their family was reportedly influenced by personal reflections on the importance of family, a sentiment shared by both parents in recent interviews.

(The above story first appeared on LatestLY on Jul 20, 2026 07:28 AM IST. For more news and updates on politics, world, sports, entertainment and lifestyle, log on to our website latestly.com).
news.summary: US Vice President JD Vance and Second Lady Usha Vance have welcomed their fourth child, Alec Neel Vance, born Sunday at Walter Reed National Military Medical Center. This marks the first time a sitting US vice president has had a child in over 150 years. Both mother and infant are healthy, and the family has expressed deep gratitude to the medical staff.
news.author: Team Latestly

news.title: Tottenham star Cristian Romero 'snubs' Trump handshake after World Cup final
news.text: Tottenham star Cristian Romero appeared to snub US President Donald Trump's handshake during a presentation of runners-up medals at the 2026 World Cup final. Spain won the World Cup after beating the defending champions 1-0 in extra time, with Ferran Torres scoring the decisive goal after Argentina were reduced to 10 men when Enzo Fernandez was sent off in the 93rd minute. But despite being subbed off by Argentina, 28-year-old defender Romero still managed to make his mark on the match by appearing to ignore Trump. In now-viral footage circulating on social media, Romero can be seen shaking the hand of both Fifa president Gianni Infantino and Mexican president Claudia Sheinbaum, who were either side of Trump. The Spurs star can also be seen glazing over the US president in the middle, as Trump helped by handing out medals to both teams. The awkward Trump moment was the third in a hat trick of awkward moments for the US President. Shortly before kick-off, Trump appeared on the giant screens inside MetLife Stadium. The cheers that had accompanied the national anthem just moments before quickly gave way to a chorus of boos, whistles, and jeers directed at the president, who had arrived via the Marine One helicopter and made a dramatic flyover of MetLife Stadium before the match got under way. Trump had been keen to avoid a repeat of his viral New York Knicks moment, while security teams left absolutely nothing to chance. F-16 fighter jets, military marksmen, and thousands of FBI agents were deployed to safeguard MetLife Stadium and the surrounding airspace. The match has been designated a "Level 1" special event - the highest security classification in the US - putting it on par with presidential inaugurations and State of the Union addresses. It didn't get much better after the match for Trump, with celebrating Spainiards briefly siding with the Argentinians in the crowd once again as it seemed the entire stadium became a cacophony of boos and jeers shortly after the last whistle of the 2026 World Cup. Footage shared on social media showed crowds heckling the 47th President of the United States as he walked across the grass to the centre of the pitch in order to present Spain with the World Cup trophy. Seemingly unbothered by the hissing crowd, Trump then refused to leave the podium after presenting Spain with the World Cup trophy, making his presence at the World Cup even more awkward. After the final whistle, the Spanish players walked onto the podium individually to collect their medals before US President Trump and Gianni Infantino presented them with the World Cup trophy. But instead of stepping aside once the presentation was over, Trump stayed on stage, shaking hands with players and raising his fists in the air as the celebrations got underway. Infantino appeared to try to usher a seemingly uncertain Trump away from the team, but the US President remained standing to the right of the squad as they celebrated, overshadowing what was meant to be Spain's trophy-lift moment.
news.summary: The Tottenham Hotspurs player was caught on camera seemingly blanking the 47th American president's offered handshake during a presentation of runners-up medals in the minutes after the last whistle at the 2026 World Cup final
news.author: Rachel Vickers-Price

news.title: JD and Usha Vance Welcome Baby Boy, First for a Sitting VP in Over 150 Years
news.text: Washington: Vice President JD Vance and his wife, second lady Usha Vance, on Sunday announced the birth of their fourth child, the first born to a sitting vice president in more than 150 years.Born Sunday, Alec Neel Vance joins older siblings Ewan, 9; Vivek, 6; and Mirabel, 4.Vance announced the birth on social media with a statement signed by him and his wife."We are excited to announce that our baby boy, Alec Neel Vance, was born this morning. Usha and the baby are happy and healthy, and our kids are overjoyed to meet their little brother," the statement said.They thanked doctors and staff at the Walter Reed National Military Medical Center and with the White House medical team.The Republican vice president's growing family is in keeping with his passionate advocacy for Americans to have more children. He has also suggested that the 2025 killing of conservative activist Charlie Kirk, who was his friend, factored into the decision to have another child.Vance, a former US Marine, repeatedly expressed alarm about declining birth rates in America as he launched his political career in 2021 with a bid for a US Senate seat from Ohio. As vice president, he said in Washington at the 2025 March for Life anti-abortion rally, "I want more babies in the United States of America."The vice president, who's 41, has been accompanied on overseas trips by Usha Vance, 40, and their children, with the kids often pajama-clad as they board Air Force Two for the overnight flights.Usha Vance, the daughter of immigrants from India, had already been the subject of some public fascination because of her husband. Her pregnancy, which was announced in January, amplified that spotlight since it is rare for occupants of the United States' highest public offices to add to their families while serving.The last time a sitting vice president became a new father was in the 1800s.Schuyler Colfax and his second wife, Ellen Wade, had a son, Schuyler Colfax III, in 1870 when Colfax was serving as vice president, according to the White House Historical Association. Decades before that, John C. Calhoun and his wife, Floride Bonneau, had a son, William, in 1829 when Calhoun was vice president, the association said.The newest addition to Vance's family was part of a recent mini baby boom among top members of President Donald Trump's administration. White House deputy chief of staff Stephen Miller's wife, Katie Miller, recently gave birth to the couple's fourth child, and White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt had her second child with her husband, Nicholas Riccio, in May.Usha Vance recently came under scrutiny after The New York Times' fashion critic called attention to the stomach-hugging, coral maternity dress she wore for a Father's Day episode of "Storytime with the Second Lady," a video podcast she launched during the pregnancy.The Times wrote that the attention that Vance, Miller and Leavitt had put on their pregnancies offered "an image of idealized womanhood that gives literal shape to the pronatalist movement," which encourages couples to have as many children as possible to counter declining birth rates.The second lady responded on social media, writing, "Now that we know the political significance of my $8.75 coral maternity dress from Old Navy, can't wait to hear what the New York Times has to say about my elastic-waistband pants and compression socks! In the meantime, enjoy my pregnancy fashion (or lack thereof) and a good story with your kids on Storytime with the Second Lady." She later shared the receipt for the dress, and her husband applauded her frugality."She bought a $50 dress for $8.75. America: meet your next director of the federal budget!" the vice president wrote on social media.JD Vance has said it was Kirk's widow, Erika Kirk, telling them that she wished she'd had more than two children with her husband before he was assassinated last September in Utah that led him and the second lady to decide to grow their family. Usha Vance, a former attorney who once clerked for Supreme Court Chief Justice John Roberts, concurred - in part."It was very powerful, what (Erika) said about her own family, and certainly very moving to both of us. I think I had already started to open my mind to the possibility," Usha Vance said during a joint interview with her husband for "CBS Sunday Morning" that aired in June. "I wouldn't say that this was, for me in any way, the decisive factor. But it came in the middle of a conversation that we were already having."

news.title: Rodri Seemingly Forces Donald Trump To Move During World Cup Trophy Celebration, Video Goes Viral: WATCH
news.text: Spain ended their 16-year World Cup drought and defeated Argentina 1-0 to become World Champions for the second time. Spain were arguably the best team of the tournament and deservedly lifted the trophy at MetLife Stadium on Sunday, July 19. But the celebrations took an unexpected turn after Spain captain Rodri's awkward moment with US President Donald Trump. In a video that has gone viral on social media, Trump remained on stage after handing the World Cup Trophy to Rodri. After waiting for a few seconds for Trump to make way, Rodri seemingly pushed him aside to clear the space before FIFA President Gianni Infantino intervened and guided Trump away, allowing the Spain players to celebrate. There were no official comments by Rodri on the awkward moment with Trump and whether he asked the President to move away, but the viral video has got the fans speculating. This is not the first time Trump stayed on the stage after handing over the trophy. During the club World Cup final last year at MetLife Stadium, Trump stayed on stage after handing over the trophy. Chelsea captain Reece James had reacted to the moment and said he felt Trump wanted to stay on the stage. "Before, they told me he was going to present the trophy, and then exit the stage. I thought that he was gonna exit the stage. But I think he wanted to stay." 'We played Out Hearts Out' - Lionel MessiMessi had a terrific World Cup, but his team faltered at the final frontier, and the Argentina captain accepted that Spain were the better team on the day, even as he failed to control his emotions and burst into tears. “I feel sad. But I am aware that we played our hearts out,” Messi said. Argentina manager Lionel Scaloni agreed. “There is sadness, but also the knowledge that we gave it our all,” Scaloni said. “I could say a lot about how we got here, but it’s not worth it. I am eternally grateful to these guys; they competed right to the end. The other team was better.”
news.summary: Donald Trump's awkward moment with Spain captain Rodri during the FIFA World Cup 2026 trophy presentation has gone viral, leaving the fans wondering if Rodri pushed Trump after the US President remained on stage during celebrations.
news.author: Faham Uddin

news.title: The 13 photos that summed up the chaos of the 2026 World Cup final
news.text:
It was a tournament where the football was magical, the off-field antics were questionable, and ultimately, Spain left the United States as the champions of the world. 

Quick hits: Argentina loses its head post-gameBlog: Spain makes history as Messi left in tears

A day after the utter chaos of the third-placed playoff between France and England, where 10 goals were scored, Argentina and Spain played things a little more conservatively — right up until the game exploded into action.

Here are the 13 photos that tell the story of the 2026 FIFA World Cup final. 

1. The Fresh Prince of Barcelona

While much of the tournament had been focused on the more established superstar names like Lionel Messi, Erling Haaland and Kylian Mbappé, teenager Lamine Yamal had floated under the radar for the World Cup passers-by.

Coming to the final having never lost a match where he had started for Spain, expectations were high on the 19-year-old as he came into the stadium. Would he be the hero that Spain needed to overcome the perceived might of Argentina?

Whatever the pressure, he arrived looking like the coolest dude on the planet, calm, collected and rocking a style straight out of The Fresh Prince of Bel-Air.

2.Smoke and mirrors

All eyes turned to the skies before kick-off as US President Donald Trump made his usual subtle entrance into the area, coming in low and fast in the Marine One helicopter.

Once settled in prime position in a corporate box on the wings, Trump was afforded the questionable chance to give the trophy a little pat as FIFA boss Gianni Infantino brought the iconic gold statue to Trump's seat.

Before that, though, there was the potentially awkward meeting between Trump and Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney, just a couple of days after Trump accused Canada of being "filthy" and "polluted" over its wildfire smoke that had blanketed New York in the lead-up to the tournament.

All-in-all things seemed reasonably diplomatic, given Mother Nature had done her job to send the smoke packing just in time for the game.

3. America makes a double Buff

In the most American moment of this entire game, event organisers decided that what this little old soccer match needed was a double Buffer.

Bruce and Michael Buffer, to be exact, the legendary ring announcers from every blood sport imaginable.

Flipping from one brother to the next, the pair excitedly announced that the game was "ready to rumble", in scenes not far off that time that soccer came to Springfield in The Simpsons.

4. The GOAT meets the rising star

Looking a little less cool than when he arrived, but still the picture of confidence, Yamal made a beeline for Lionel Messi before the match to wish arguably the greatest player in history some luck.

It was a full circle moment for the pair, who originally met on a photo shoot in 2007 when Yamal's parents won a Unicef raffle to have Yamal photographed for a calendar with the then rising star Messi.

This time around Messi's hair was a little shorter and his face a little more wearied, while Yamal was a little less nude.

5. The World Cup's Mariah Carey

Shakira was rolled out for the halftime show, with the Colombian star fast becoming to the World Cup what Mariah Carey is to Christmas.

She's just always there.

While her performance was fantastic, the rest of it — from Justin Bieber to Madonna to BTS — left a little bit to be desired.

So much so that English footballing champion Wayne Rooney didn't hold back with what he thought.

"I thought it was crap," he said on the BBC coverage.

6. The spectacular red card

As soon as we all saw it happen on the TV, we knew that the photos were going to be spectacular.

And veteran Getty photographer Al Bello didn't let us down.

Rising Spanish star Pau Cubarsi was sent flying by Argentina's Enzo Fernandez, but it was made all the more spectacular by Cubarsi really leaning into the flip.

Had Fernandez not already had a yellow, it's unlikely he would have been sent off at all, but the red card made it all the more dramatic as the scores stood at 0-0 heading into the death.

7. Hydration break goes to a new level

Two things have been major talking points during this tournament — it's been bloody hot and the hydration breaks have been bloody irritating. 

So it was fun to see the two come together in the final regular time break of the tournament, as the Spanish players took advantage of the sprinklers to cool off.

Was it the trick that won them the World Cup? Who's to say. But almost certainly yes.

8. Free kick wipes a goal

In a moment of brilliance, Nico Williams looked to have won it for Spain when he slotted a goal past the keeper to make it 1-0 late.

But the referee called it back. There was a free kick somewhere in the mess of bodies in the box.

In live action it wasn't immediately clear where the free kick was. On replay, it still wasn't totally clear.

But on the fifth or sixth watch — yeah, no idea.

Williams was furious, and rightfully so.

9. Deadlock ends in headlock

Go back to the very first photo at the top of this story and you can experience the majesty of the Ferran Torres goal that broke the game open.

Hair blowing in the breeze, his teammates running in behind him, the fans screaming and cheering in ecstasy.

And then Williams decided to choke Torres for some reason.

Angry that he wasn't the hero after his goal was wiped? An in-joke that we don't know about?

Who knows. But it made for an odd scene in an incredible moment.

10. Tears of a legend

Small in stature but grand in legacy, Messi cut a forlorn figure after the final whistle.

His tiny body huddled in the middle of the massive stadium was enough to make anyone feel sorry for the attacking superstar.

His place as the greatest player the game has ever seen would have been secured had he managed to drag his teammates to victory, but even Messi couldn't get his countrymen through the incredible defensive wall that Spain had built.

11. Infantino chases after Trump

Trump had made headlines this time last year when he joined Chelsea on stage for the celebrations in the Club World Cup final, despite the apparent confusion of the players.

This time there was surely no way that Trump would be caught right in the middle of the pack as the Spanish players lifted the most coveted trophy in world sport, right?

Well, kind of. This time Trump wandered right across stage as Infantino pivoted left, leaving the FIFA boss to scramble after the US president, with the pair awkwardly caught in the team celebration photos.

On the broadcast, you could hear someone say "they'll crop him out" in an unexpected, but funny, hot mic moment.

12. Utter heartache for Argentina

Goalkeeper Emiliano Martinez had been immense through the World Cup.

But as Spain lifted the trophy, the Aston Villa keeper let the emotions flow, caught by the camera as he sat on the sidelines thinking about what might have been.

It was a reminder of what this thing, this silly, epic, immense mess of a game, means to the people who play it.

13. The love of a mum

In the end, all we really want is the hug of a mum who's proud of you.

And that's what Yamal got as his mum, Sheila, ran onto the ground to give her son a big old cuddle.

Having given birth at just 16 years old as an immigrant from Equatorial Guinea, Sheila's story is the one that really captures the essence of the World Cup.

Not the championship rings or corporate boxes. Not the powerful men in powerful suits making powerful political moves.

A mum with a dream for her son to shine on the world stage.

This was the moment that really mattered.
news.summary: It was magical chaos from start to finish as Spain claimed victory in the 2026 World Cup final. Here are the 13 photos that summed it up.
news.author: Kyle Pollard

news.title: US launches ninth night of strikes on Iran as it hits out at Gulf neighbors
news.text:
TEHRAN: New violence shook the Middle East early Monday as the United States announced another wave of attacks on Iran, Kuwait said it was intercepting drones and Tehran’s Revolutionary Guards claimed to have hit an “enemy command centre” in Syria.

Bahrain’s interior ministry also said that its air raid sirens had sounded and urged citizens and residents to head to “the nearest safe place”.

US Central Command (CENTCOM) late Sunday announced it was beginning a wave of strikes on Iran for the ninth night in a row.

The attacks were aimed at “degrading Iranian military capabilities used to attack commercial vessels and civilian mariners” in the strategic Strait of Hormuz, the post on X said.

A preliminary deal aimed at ending the war has collapsed as the foes fight to break a deadlock over the Strait of Hormuz, a waterway key to the world’s oil supply.

Since the start of the war on February 28, Iran has struck back by targeting US allies and military assets in its own neighborhood, and by virtually blocking the strait through which a fifth of world oil supplies transited prior to the war.

Kuwaiti air defences were “engaging hostile drone attacks following Iranian aggression”, its army said early Monday. “The General Staff of the Army notes that any explosions heard are a result of air defense systems intercepting the hostile attacks,” a post on the official X account of the Kuwait Army said.

Iran’s Revolutionary Guards said Monday they had launched a “surprise attack” on an enemy command centre in Syria, in a statement reported by state news agency IRNA.

They announced “a surprise attack on the enemy special operations command centre in the Al-Tanf region of Syria in revenge for the blood of martyred soldiers of Iranshahr”, a city in southeast Iran, in an IRNA statement posted on Telegram.

Iran said Sunday it had targeted two US bases in Kuwait in response to more than a week of intensifying attacks, which Tehran says have hit transport and infrastructure sites, including an under-construction nuclear power plant.

Jordan also reported intercepting missiles on Sunday.

Casualty counts

The fresh rounds of violence came after a weekend in which the US announced three new US military fatalities and one service member missing in action.

Two were killed during Iranian missile and drone attacks on Jordan Friday, while another service member died in northern Iraq during the controlled detonation of an unexploded ordnance from a downed Iranian drone, CENTCOM said.

President Donald Trump said Sunday the latest US strikes on Iran “hit them very hard again tonight” in honor of the American fatalities.

The deaths brought to 17 the confirmed number of American military fatalities since the US and Israel launched the war with a wave of strikes on Iranian targets on February 28.

That toll is dwarfed by Iran’s reported casualties. Its health ministry said Friday that 50 people had been killed in recent fighting and more than 500 hurt.

The renewed violence was initially sparked by Iranian attacks on ships in the strategic Strait of Hormuz.

Iran closed the conduit for oil and gas shipments after the war broke out, and control over it has become leverage in its negotiations with Washington, which has reimposed its own blockade on Iranian ports.

On Sunday, the Revolutionary Guards said two ships attempting to transit the strait were stopped.

“Four violating ships, with the mischief and support of American terrorists…intended to disrupt and exit the Strait of Hormuz via an unsafe route, two of which had an accident and were stopped in place,” the Guards said, adding that the other two ships abandoned their journey.

Hope for a political settlement to the war has dimmed, though mediators have attempted to bring both sides back to the negotiating table.

Iran’s central military commander, Ali Abdollahi, warned that further US aggression would face a “decisive and devastating response”, according to a statement carried by state television on Sunday.

The Atomic Energy Organization of Iran said the United States had attacked the unfinished Darkhovin nuclear power plant in the country’s southwest, calling it “an aggressive and brutal act contrary to international law”.

The UN’s nuclear watchdog said it was looking into the report, noting the plant “contained no nuclear material when last visited by the IAEA”. Nonetheless, IAEA director Rafael Grossi reiterated his “call for military restraint in the vicinity of all nuclear-related sites”, the agency said in a post on X.
news.summary:
TEHRAN: New violence shook the Middle East early Monday as the United States announced another wave of attacks on Iran, Kuwait said it was intercepting drones and Tehran’s Revolutionary Guards claimed to have hit an “enemy command centre” in Syria.


Bahrain’s interior ministry also said that its air raid sirens had sounded and urged citizens and residents to head to “the nearest safe place”.


US Central Command (CENTCOM) late Sunday announced it was beginning a wave of strikes on Iran for the ninth night in a row.


The attacks were aimed at “degrading Iranian military capabilities used to attack commercial vessels and civilian mariners” in the strategic Strait of Hormuz, the post on X said.

news.title: Lionel Messi's emotional tears after likely final World Cup leave fans heartbroken
news.text:
Lionel Messi was overcome with emotion after Argentina's World Cup dream came to a heartbreaking end on Sunday, with the football legend breaking down in tears following Spain's 1-0 extra-time victory in New Jersey.

In what is widely expected to be the 39-year-old's final appearance at a FIFA World Cup, the Argentina captain struggled to inspire his side before being left devastated as Spain lifted football's biggest prize.

The emotional scenes unfolded after the final whistle at MetLife Stadium, where an estimated 1.7 billion people around the world watched the dramatic showdown.

As soon as the referee brought the match to an end, Messi sank to the turf, covering his face with his hands as the magnitude of the moment became clear.

Teammates and opponents attempted to console the eight-time Ballon d'Or winner, but the emotion was evident as he contemplated what is likely to be the final chapter of his remarkable World Cup journey.

The heartbreak continued during the medal ceremony. After receiving his runners-up medal from President Donald Trump, Messi struggled to contain his emotions, bursting into tears moments before Spain lifted the World Cup trophy.

He later stood in front of Argentina's travelling supporters, visibly emotional as they applauded their captain following another memorable tournament.

The defeat brought to an end Messi's third appearance in a World Cup final. His first came in Brazil in 2014, when Argentina were beaten 1-0 by Germany after extra time.

Eight years later, he finally realised his lifelong dream by leading Argentina to World Cup glory in Qatar, cementing his place among football's all-time greats.

This year's tournament was expected to be one final opportunity for the Inter Miami star to add another title before bringing his international career to a close.

By the time the next World Cup arrives, Messi will be 43, making another appearance highly unlikely.

Although his club career is set to continue with Inter Miami, Sunday's final is expected to mark the end of one of international football's greatest careers.

Facing a country where he enjoyed so much success during his years with Barcelona, Messi was unable to produce his trademark moments of magic.

Spain's energetic defence limited his influence throughout the contest, with the likes of teenage sensation Lamine Yamal helping keep Argentina's talisman unusually quiet.

The decisive moment came deep into extra time when Ferran Torres found the breakthrough, handing Spain the World Cup title and leaving Argentina to settle for silver.

Supporters around the world were quick to express their admiration for the Argentine icon following the emotional scenes.

One fan wrote on X: "You aren't a football fan if you don't feel sad for Messi."

Another posted: "Thanks for everything you put into this game. You'll always be the best."

A third added: "The greatest of all time. The best to ever do it."

While Messi's tournament ended in disappointment, France star Kylian Mbappé celebrated a major personal milestone after securing the Golden Boot.

The Real Madrid forward finished the competition with 10 goals, two ahead of Messi, who was unable to close the gap in the final.

The achievement makes Mbappé the first player in World Cup history to win the Golden Boot twice, having also claimed the award at the 2022 tournament in Qatar, further underlining his status as one of football's brightest stars.
news.summary: Lionel Messi was overcome with emotion after Argentina's World Cup final defeat to Spain in what is expected to be the football icon's final appearance on the sport's biggest stage.
news.author: Faye James

news.title: Imran’s sister to face NCCIA probe over ‘false statement’
news.text:
Summoned to appear on July 20; move follows video containing ‘orchestrated conflict’ claims
• Attaullah Tarar accuses PTI of damaging Pakistan’s image for political gain, claims ‘India exploited her remarks’ 

ISLAMABAD/LAHORE: The National Cyber Crime Investigation Agency (NCCIA) has summoned Noreen Niazi, the sister of incarcerated former PM Imran Khan, to appear on Monday over allegations of disseminating “false, offensive and inflammatory” content against state institutions.

“You have been found to have disseminated false, offensive and inflammatory social media content to defame state institutions and spread fake narratives,” an NCCIA notice dated July 18 stated.

The agency directed Ms Ni--a---zi to appear at its Isla-m--abad office on July 20 in con---n--ection with an inquiry. “In case of non-compliance, it will be assumed that you have nothing to present or state in your defence,” the notice further stated, adding that non-compliance was punishable under Section 174 of the Pakistan Penal Code.

While the notice did not specify the content, it was issued after a clip of her making claims about last year’s brief military conflict between Pakistan and India was widely shared on social media.

In the clip, Ms Niazi is seen alleging during an interview that the conflict was “orchestrated” to improve the image of Pakistan’s military.

She claimed the “drama” was played out in collaboration with Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi, questioning why Modi would have refrained from further attacking Pakistan otherwise.

Ms Niazi went on to allege that the objective was to get Israel recognised by Pakistan, further linking US President Donald Trump’s praise of Pakistan’s leadership with Israel’s recognition as well.

Minister slams remarks

Meanwhile, Federal Inf-o------r--m--ation Minister Attau-llah Tarar accused Ms Niazi of making remarks that were “amplified by the Indian media”, alleging that the opposition was attempting to malign Pakistan for political gains.

Addressing a ceremony in Lahore, Mr Tarar said Ms Niazi’s statements sparked “strong public anger” and claimed they had been used by Indian media against Pakistan.

He asserted that history would remember any attempt to defame the country and that those seeking to tarnish Pakistan’s image have always faced humiliation.

The minister alleged that the opposition leadership had failed to criticise governance issues in Khy-ber Pakhtunkhwa, focus--ing instead on negative politics.

“Pakistan exists; therefore, everything exists,” Tarar said, adding that the country’s international standing was improving.

Published in Dawn, July 20th, 2026

news.title: Did Rodri Push US President Donald Trump Aside During Spains FIFA World Cup 2026 Celebration? Viral Video
news.text: Spain captain Rodri and US President Donald Trump were involved in an awkward moment during the FIFA World Cup 2026 trophy celebration after Spain beat Argentina 1-0. As Rodri prepared to lift the trophy, he appeared to gesture and speak towards Trump, prompting online speculation that he asked him to move aside. Rodri has not officially commented on the exchange.
news.author: Amertha Rangankar
